title: Repairing a damaged door frame
subtitle: Splice a new section into a rotted or split door frame without replacing the whole jamb
excerpt: How to cut out a damaged section of a door frame and splice in a matching piece. Covers the diagonal scarf cut, glue choice for an exterior versus interior splice, fixing into the wall, and finishing flush with the surrounding frame.
sourceNotes: Newey & Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual.

glossaryTerms.definition: A diagonal joint between two pieces of timber that gives more glue surface than a butt joint and stays hidden once finished. Standard splice for repairing damaged section of skirting, architrave, and door frames.

content.text: A door frame splits at the latch plate or rots at the bottom corner where rain blows in. Replacing the whole jamb means lifting the door, cutting back the architrave, and disturbing the plaster. A
scarf joint
splices in a new section of timber and leaves the door, architrave, and plaster in place.
Step 1, mark the cut lines

Find a point above the damaged section where the timber is sound. Mark a diagonal line across the face of the frame at 45 degrees. Find another sound point below the damaged section and mark a second diagonal at 45 degrees in the opposite direction. The two cuts together remove the damaged area as a diamond-shaped piece.
Step 2, make the cuts

Saw down each line with the tenon saw, keeping the blade square to the face of the frame. Stop the cut at the back face of the frame; do not cut through into the wall behind. Use the chisel to break out the damaged piece, then pare the cut faces clean and flat.
Step 3, cut the splice piece

Choose a length of timber the same width, thickness, and species as the frame. Mark the diagonals on each end to match the cuts in the frame. Saw the diagonals, holding the saw at the same angle as the frame cuts. Test-fit the splice into the frame opening. Pare any high spots until it slides in tight without forcing.
Step 4, glue and fit

Apply waterproof PVA glue to both diagonal faces on an interior frame, or polyurethane glue on an exterior frame. Press the splice into place. Tap it flush with the surrounding frame using a hammer and a softwood block to protect the face. Drive two pilot-and-screw fixings through the splice into the wall behind, hidden where the architrave will cover them. Wipe excess glue with a damp cloth.
Step 5, let the glue cure

Allow the glue to cure for the time on the bottle. PVA stands at 8 hours and is fully cured by 24 hours. Polyurethane stands at 4 hours and is fully cured by 24 hours. Do not load the splice (no hanging the door, no banging) during the cure.
Step 6, plane and sand flush

Plane the face of the splice flush with the surrounding frame. A small block plane is the right tool. Take fine shavings; the splice should sit perfectly level with the original timber. Sand the joint area with 120 grit then 180 grit on the sanding block.
Step 7, refinish to match

Match the original frame finish. A painted frame takes a primer plus two coats of paint. A stained frame takes the same stain as the original; mix from a small test pot to match the existing colour, then varnish. The scarf joint will be visible as a faint line across the timber once finished; a clear coat reads as a maker's mark, a paint coat hides it completely.
Variations

On a frame where the rot is only at the bottom 100 mm, a single 45-degree cut is enough; the splice slides up under the cut from below. On a hardwood frame, the cuts and the splice should both be the same hardwood. Mixing softwood splice into a hardwood frame leaves a soft spot that wears proud of the surrounding timber.
Care

A correctly spliced frame lasts as long as the rest of the frame. On an exterior frame, the splice area is the natural weak point; renew the paint or stain every two years and inspect for re-rot at the joint line. A small spot of re-rot can be cut out and re-spliced with the same method.
Troubleshooter

attrs.symptom: Gap at one face of the scarf joint after gluing
attrs.cause: The cuts were not square to the frame face, or the splice was forced in.
attrs.fix: Fill the gap with a colour-matched wood filler before the glue cures. For a precision repair, cut a new splice and start again.

attrs.symptom: Splice rots within a year on an exterior frame
attrs.cause: Untreated softwood used in an exterior splice, or the joint line was not sealed against water.
attrs.fix: Cut out the new rot. Splice in a pressure-treated section or a hardwood. Seal the joint line with a clear silicone before re-painting.
